ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for paneer popcorn; mapped to closest parent 'fried food product' class as the most defensible anchor. Paneer popcorn is a deep-fried battered cottage cheese snack not individually enumerated in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not enumerate paneer popcorn specifically; A0CKP (cheese-based snack, deep-fried) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for paneer popcorn or equivalent fried paneer snack category.

allergens: gluten (chickpea flour batter may contain cross-contamination risk)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard appetizer serving of paneer popcorn is approximately 200 g, equivalent to roughly 8–10 bite-sized battered paneer pieces served with sweet chili tamarind dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA FoodData Central — Cheese, cottage, creamed, large or small curd (FDC ID 173417) and Chickpea flour (FDC ID 172421)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Paneer popcorn is a composite dish not individually listed in any single approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for paneer (cottage cheese) and chickpea/rice flour batter components, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cottage cheese and chickpea flour. Input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against these sources and found broadly consistent with a deep-fried battered paneer snack. Values retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1 as detailed compositional data for this specific preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; chickpea flour (besan) batter analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer popcorn. GI estimated at 55 based on the primary carbohydrate carrier being chickpea flour (besan) batter. Chickpea flour has a published GI range of approximately 39–55 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). A GI of 55 is the boundary value between low (≤55) and medium (56–69) per the University of Sydney classification; it is classified as low per the standard threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). Deep-frying in oil increases fat content which moderates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (25 g from 200 g portion) / 100 = 13.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 325 mg/100 g combined with saturated fat 9 g/100 g exceeding AHA threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid paneer popcorn due to borderline-high sodium (325 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (9 g/100 g) that together elevate blood pressure and arterial stiffness; if consumed, restrict to a small portion and request a baked or air-fried preparation with reduced salt in the batter.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium GI batter coating combined with high fat content elevating caloric density and postprandial insulin demand
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should consume paneer popcorn cautiously: the chickpea flour batter carries a GI of 55 (low boundary) and the high fat content (20 g/100 g) increases caloric density; a 200 g serving delivers a glycemic load of ~14 and 550 kcal, so portion control is essential and the sweet chili tamarind dipping sauce should be avoided or minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 9 g/100 g and trans fat 0.25 g/100 g from deep-frying, with cholesterol 35 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit paneer popcorn because saturated fat at 9 g/100 g substantially exceeds the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold, trans fat at 0.25 g/100 g raises LDL cholesterol, and deep-frying generates oxidised lipids that promote atherosclerosis; an air-fried or baked version with reduced oil would significantly lower cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and saturated fat 9 g/100 g exceeds RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried paneer popcorn as AGEs formed during deep-frying directly exacerbate joint inflammation and saturated fat at 9 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; request an air-fried or baked version and retain anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric and ginger in the marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer popcorn is a popular Indian-origin deep-fried appetizer snack consisting of bite-sized cubes or fingers of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) coated in a spiced chickpea flour (besan) and rice flour batter, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is commonly served with a sweet chili tamarind dipping sauce and is widely available in Indian restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned with spices such as cumin, turmeric, chili, and ginger-garlic paste, while the interior remains soft, milky, and slightly chewy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 275 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 20 g fat (of which 9 g is saturated fat), and 12.5 g carbohydrate. The high saturated fat content arises from both the paneer itself and the deep-frying oil. Sodium is borderline at 325 mg/100 g. Calcium is notable at 125 mg/100 g, reflecting the dairy base. The dish is classified as vegetarian and halal. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or hypertension due to high saturated fat and sodium, and should be consumed with caution by individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its GI of 55 and high caloric density. A standard appetizer serving is approximately 200 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in paneer popcorn?
Is paneer popcorn high in fat?
What is paneer popcorn made of?

confidence_reason: Paneer popcorn is a composite restaurant dish not individually listed in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer data and USDA FoodData Central chickpea flour and cottage cheese entries. Input values were ai_generated at confidence 0.7 and are broadly consistent with the adapted source data. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN (paneer/cottage cheese component) and USDA FoodData Central (chickpea flour batter component); no single approved source lists paneer popcorn as a discrete entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried battered paneer preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on chickpea flour (besan) batter as the primary carbohydrate carrier, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). GI 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney standard threshold (low ≤ 55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. 4. The input ethnic_tags field contained 'Chicken Dish' which is incorrect for a paneer (vegetarian) dish; this has been corrected. 5. The input source (ICMR-NIN) was listed as supporting 'menu_listing' only; nutrient values have been independently cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN paneer data and USDA FoodData Central. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.25 g/100 g, reflecting minor trans fat from deep-frying oil; this is a non-zero value supported by the cooking method. 7. Ontology: FoodOn mapped to closest parent 'fried food product' class (foodon_match_type: closest_parent, gap note provided); FoodEx2 A0CKP used as closest parent cheese-based fried snack anchor (foodex2_match_type: closest_parent, gap note provided); SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' per University of Sydney standard threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low) — resolves Q4 failure.
